title: Set Up the Observer Agent
lede: Give every call a silent supervisor: the Observer Agent is a second AI that watches the conversation in real time, flags critical moments the instant they happen, and can even trigger background tools — all without slowing down the main agent by a single millisecond.

steps.title: Open the Agent You Want to Monitor
steps.content:
The Observer Agent is configured per agent, so each of your Smart Agents can watch for different things — your sales agent can flag buying signals while your support agent flags frustration.

  1. In the left sidebar, click Intelligence Center → Smart Agents.
  2. Click the name of the agent you want to add an observer to. The agent editor opens.

images.alt: Smart Agents list under Intelligence Center
images.caption: Open Intelligence Center → Smart Agents and click an agent's name.

images.alt: Smart Agents list under Intelligence Center
images.caption: Open Intelligence Center → Smart Agents and click an agent's name.

steps.title: Turn On the Observer Agent
steps.content:
In the agent editor (Form view), the middle column lists every capability your agent can have — Knowledge Base, Voice (TTS), Webhooks Post Processing, and more.

  1. Find the Observer Agent row (bell icon).
  2. Click the OFF pill on the right to flip it to ON. A default model is selected for you automatically.
  3. Click anywhere on the Observer Agent row label to open its configuration window.
💡 Using the Node view instead? The same Observer Agent card lives in the capabilities bar there — click it to open the identical configuration window. The Observer chip under the agent's name at the top of the editor also lights up whenever the observer is active.

images.alt: Agent editor with the Observer Agent row toggled ON
images.caption: The Observer Agent row in the capability list — toggle it ON, then click the row to configure.

images.alt: Agent editor with the Observer Agent row toggled ON
images.caption: The Observer Agent row in the capability list — toggle it ON, then click the row to configure.

steps.title: Configure the Model and Tools
steps.content:
The Observer Agent window opens with a Configuration card across the top and your event list on the left. The Configuration card has three controls:

  • LLM Model — the language model that powers the observer. Because it listens in the background and never speaks, you can afford a slower, more thorough model here without adding any latency to the live conversation. A fast, cost-efficient default is pre-selected when you enable the observer.
  • MCP Server (optional) — connect an MCP server and the observer can silently trigger external tools when an event fires (log a ticket, post to Slack, update a record) while the main agent keeps talking. Leave it empty if you only want event flags.
  • ON / OFF pill — the master switch. When it's OFF, everything below is grayed out and the observer never joins a call.
💡 No MCP servers in the list yet? Create one first under Intelligence Center → Automation, then come back and pick it here.

images.alt: Observer Agent configuration window with model picker, MCP picker, and events list
images.caption: The Configuration card on top, the Events rail on the left, and the editor pane on the right.

images.alt: Observer Agent configuration window with model picker, MCP picker, and events list
images.caption: The Configuration card on top, the Events rail on the left, and the editor pane on the right.

steps.title: Review the 10 Built-In Events
steps.content:
Events are the moments the observer watches for. The Events rail on the left comes pre-loaded with 10 battle-tested defaults, each with its own color and an individual ON / OFF pill:

  • Call Quality Issues — audio degrades, the connection drops, or technical problems occur.
  • Customer Frustration Detected — signs of anger or dissatisfaction.
  • Escalation Request — the caller asks for a human agent or supervisor.
  • Payment Information Discussed — sensitive financial details are being shared.
  • Compliance Violation Risk — the conversation nears regulated or policy-restricted topics.
  • High-Value Opportunity — strong interest in premium products or large purchases.
  • Competitor Mention — the caller names a competing product or company.
  • Confusion or Misunderstanding — the caller seems lost on pricing, details, or next steps.
  • Objection Handling Needed — concerns or objections that need a specialized response.
  • Call Duration Exceeded — the call runs past optimal length.

Turn off any event you don't need with its pill, or remove it entirely with the trash icon (a confirmation asks first). The counter at the top of the rail (e.g. 10/10) shows how many events are active. Deleted or mangled the defaults? Click Reset to Defaults at the bottom of the rail to restore all 10.

steps.title: Fine-Tune an Event
steps.content:
Click any event in the left rail to open its editor on the right. Three fields define how it behaves:

  1. Event Name — the label shown on Live Calls and in reports. Keep it short and scannable.
  2. Description — this is the important one: it's the instruction the observer's AI actually follows. Describe exactly when the event should fire, e.g. "Triggered when customer explicitly asks to speak with a human agent or supervisor".
  3. Color — pick from the color swatch or the Quick select palette. This color tints the call's card and column on the Live Calls board, so choose red/orange tones for urgent events and green/blue for positive ones.

Changes save automatically as you type. The header of the editor also shows a DEFAULT badge for built-in events, plus the event's own ON / OFF pill.

⚠️ Vague descriptions produce noisy flags. "Customer is unhappy" fires constantly; "Customer uses angry language, threatens to cancel, or says they want a refund" fires exactly when you need it.

images.alt: Event editor showing name, description, and color for the Escalation Request event
images.caption: Select an event on the left to edit its name, trigger description, and color.

images.alt: Event editor showing name, description, and color for the Escalation Request event
images.caption: Select an event on the left to edit its name, trigger description, and color.

steps.title: Add Your Own Custom Events
steps.content:
The defaults are just the start — the real power is teaching the observer to watch for the moments that matter to your business.

  1. Click + Add Event at the bottom of the Events rail.
  2. Enter an Event Name (e.g. "Warranty Claim Mentioned").
  3. Write a precise Description of when it should trigger.
  4. Pick an Event Color and click Add.

The new event appears in the rail, enabled and selected, ready for further edits. When you're done with everything, click Done to close the window — all changes are already saved.

images.alt: Add New Event dialog with name, description, and color fields
images.caption: Create a custom event with its own trigger description and color.

images.alt: Add New Event dialog with name, description, and color fields
images.caption: Create a custom event with its own trigger description and color.

steps.title: Watch It Work on Live Calls
steps.content:
This is the payoff: a live wallboard where flagged conversations jump out at you the moment something happens.

  1. In the top navigation, click Contact Center, then open the Live Calls tab (or click Call Logs → Live Calls from the sidebar's Call Center section).
  2. The stat strip at the top shows Live Calls, Dialing, Ongoing, Transferred, Events Flagged, and Finished — the Events Flagged tile counts every observer detection across your active calls.
  3. When the observer fires an event mid-call, the call's card is tinted with the event's color and tagged with the event name. Hover the tag to see the observer's reasoning and its confidence score.
  • In Tabs layout, each active event gets its own tab (matching the kanban columns), so all "Escalation Request" calls line up together.
  • In the table view, the Observer Event column shows the latest flag per call, and flagged rows get a light tint in the event's color.
  • Use the observer event filter above the board to show only calls with specific events — perfect for a supervisor triaging frustration flags.

images.alt: Live Calls page with the Events Flagged tile and Observer Event column
images.caption: Live Calls: the Events Flagged tile and the Observer Event column light up as the observer detects events.

images.alt: Live Calls page with the Events Flagged tile and Observer Event column
images.caption: Live Calls: the Events Flagged tile and the Observer Event column light up as the observer detects events.

steps.title: Good to Know: How the Observer Behaves
steps.content:
• It never speaks. The observer is invisible to the caller — it only watches, flags, and (if you connected an MCP server) acts in the background.
  • Zero latency added. It runs in parallel with the main agent, so even a heavyweight model won't slow down responses.
  • Flags persist after the call. Detected events are stored with the call record, so you can review them later in your call history.
  • Per-agent setup. Enabling the observer on one agent doesn't affect the others — configure each with the events that fit its job.

That's it — from now on, no critical moment slips by unnoticed. Your team sees escalations, frustration, and hot opportunities the second they happen, on every single call.

support.content:

metaTitle: Set Up the Observer Agent | iMash Documentation
metaDescription: Run a background AI that monitors live iMash calls, flags events like customer frustration and escalation requests, and silently triggers tools. Enable it, tune its events, and watch flags appear on Live Calls.
